questions.prompt: What is the security risk of setting innerHTML with user-controlled data?
options: Performance overhead
XSS — the browser executes injected script tags
DOM reordering
Memory leak
questions.explanation: innerHTML parses HTML including <script> tags. User input set via innerHTML can execute arbitrary JavaScript (XSS). Use textContent instead.

options: Adds runtime validation
Tells TypeScript the return type is HTMLInputElement instead of Element
Filters only input elements in the DOM
It has no effect — it's just documentation
questions.explanation: The generic parameter gives TypeScript the specific type, granting access to input-specific properties like .value without casting.

options: Each element has one listener delegated to the browser
One listener on a parent handles events from descendant children via bubbling
Listeners are shared between sibling elements
The browser delegates event handling to Web Workers
questions.explanation: Events bubble up to parent elements. Attaching one listener to a container handles clicks on all descendants, including dynamically added ones.

questions.prompt: element.removeEventListener('click', handler) — when does this work?
options: Always, for any click handler on the element
Only when handler is the exact same function reference as the one added
Only for the first registered listener
After calling element.clearListeners() first
questions.explanation: removeEventListener requires an identical function reference. Anonymous inline functions () => {} cannot be removed.

options: Returns the first child matching .container
Returns the next sibling matching .container
Returns the nearest ancestor (including self) matching .container
Returns all .container elements in the document
questions.explanation: closest() traverses ancestors upward from the element, returning the first match (or null).

questions.prompt: Write event delegation code on a #todo-list ul that logs the text content of any clicked li.
